title: The Veteran's Kundalini: PTSD, Combat Trauma, and Spiritual Emergency
meta_description: You're probably reading this because you're scared. Maybe you're shaking, sweating, your heart's doing a drum solo in your chest. You've seen things, done
body:
The Veteran's Kundalini: PTSD, Combat Trauma, and Spiritual Emergency

You're probably reading this because you're scared. Maybe you're shaking, sweating, your heart's doing a drum solo in your chest. You've seen things, done things, felt things that no one should ever have to. And now, something else is happening. Something... spiritual? But it feels less like a blessing and more like a goddamn curse. You're not alone. I've been there, staring at the ceiling at 3 AM, wondering if I was losing my mind, or if the mind I had was never really mine to begin with. This isn't some New Age fluff. This is real, raw, and it can feel like your world is ending. But here's the thing: it's not the end. It's a beginning, a brutal, terrifying, beautiful beginning, whether you asked for it or not.

For veterans, especially those with combat trauma or PTSD, what people call "spiritual awakening" can hit different. It's not just some gentle unfolding. It's often a goddamn explosion. You've already got your nervous system wired for danger, your psyche shredded by experiences most people can't even imagine. Then, this energy, this kundalini, whatever you wanna call it, starts to move. And it doesn't ask for permission. It just goes. Right? It rips through you, stirring up everything you've buried, everything you've fought to forget. It's not pretty. It's not calm. It's a storm, and you're right in the eye of it.

I know what you're thinking. "Spiritual? I'm just trying to survive the next hour without losing my shit." And you're right to think that. The labels don't matter as much as the experience. But understanding what's happening can give you a sliver of control, a tiny foothold in the chaos. What you're feeling might be what's called a spiritual emergency. It's not a mental illness, even though it can feel exactly like one. It's a crisis of consciousness, a forced evolution. And for veterans, the pre-existing trauma makes it a thousand times more intense. It's like trying to run a marathon with a broken leg, then having someone light you on fire. It's that kind of intense.

The Trauma Reactor: Why Veterans Are Different

Look, your body isn't just a body. It's a record keeper. Every shock, every near-miss, every horrific sight - it's all stored. Combat trauma isn't just in your head; it's in your cells, your nervous system, your very DNA. You've been through hell, and that hell changes you at a fundamental level. Your fight-or-flight response is on overdrive. Your sense of safety is shattered. You learned to live in a world where danger is constant, where trust is a luxury you can't afford. Right? This isn't just "stress." This is a deep, primal wounding.

When kundalini begins to stir, it's like throwing a match into a dry forest. All that stored trauma, all those suppressed emotions, all the rage, the grief, the fear - it gets ignited. It doesn't just bubble up gently. It erupts. You might experience flashbacks that are more vivid than ever, panic attacks that make you think you're having a heart attack, or waves of emotion that are so overwhelming you feel like you're drowning. Your sleep might be a battlefield of nightmares, or you might not sleep at all. Your body might twitch, shake, burn, or feel like it's vibrating out of control. Does that sound familiar? It's not just PTSD. It's PTSD amplified by a spiritual force that's trying to clean house, whether you're ready for it or not.

I remember lying awake, heart pounding, feeling like I was being electrocuted from the inside out. Every single memory, every single wound, it was all there, playing on a loop, but with a new, terrifying intensity. It wasn't just remembering; it was reliving. And then, on top of that, these weird sensations - buzzing in my head, heat moving up my spine, involuntary movements. I thought I was losing it. I thought I was finally breaking. But I wasn't. It was the process. It was the fire burning through the dross. It's the most painful purification you'll ever go through, but it's purification nonetheless.

The Overwhelmed Nervous System

Your nervous system, bless its heart, has been through enough. It's been operating in survival mode for God knows how long. Now, imagine a powerful current running through those already frayed wires. That's what kundalini can feel like for someone with a history of trauma. You're already hyper-vigilant, right? Already sensitive to sounds, lights, crowds. Now, you're sensitive to everything, including your own internal energy. It's like your internal volume knob got cranked to eleven, and you can't turn it down.

This can manifest as extreme anxiety, paranoia, or even brief psychotic-like episodes where you feel completely disconnected from reality. You might hear sounds that aren't there, see flashes of light, or feel sensations that defy explanation. These aren't necessarily signs of mental illness in the traditional sense, though they can certainly mimic them. They're often signs of your nervous system being overloaded, trying to process an immense amount of energy and information it's not used to handling. It's like your brain is a computer trying to run a program that's too powerful for its hardware. It crashes, it glitches, it screams. But it's trying to upgrade, you know? It just doesn't feel like an upgrade when you're in the middle of it.

The key here is understanding that while these symptoms are distressing, they're often temporary and part of the process. It doesn't make them any less terrifying in the moment, I get that. But knowing what's happening can prevent you from labeling yourself as permanently broken. You're not broken. You're being rewired. It's just a damn messy, painful process.

What the Hell Is Happening? (Kundalini & Spiritual Emergency)

So what is this "kundalini" everyone talks about? Honestly, no one really knows. It's described as a coiled serpent energy at the base of the spine, a dormant spiritual power. When it awakens, it moves up the central channel of the body, clearing blockages, purifying the system, and leading to higher states of consciousness. Sounds great on paper, right? In reality, it can feel like you're being ripped apart from the inside out. It's not a gentle breeze; it's a hurricane. And for someone who's already been through a war, it's another war, this time inside your own damn body.

This is where the term Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link) comes in. It's a term coined by Dr. Stanislav Grof and Christina Grof. It describes a non-ordinary state of consciousness that's intense and overwhelming, often mistaken for psychosis or other mental disorders. But it's not pathology; it's a process of deep transformation. You're not going crazy; you're just experiencing a forced spiritual growth spurt that's tearing down the old you to build a new one. It's like your soul is saying, "No more pretending. No more hiding. We're doing this."

Symptoms can include:

  • Intense heat or cold sensations, burning, tingling, vibrations
  • Involuntary body movements, tremors, spasms
  • Visions, auditory hallucinations, out-of-body experiences
  • Episodes of intense fear, anxiety, depression, or euphoria
  • Feeling disconnected from your body or reality
  • Memory resurfacing, often traumatic, with extreme emotional charge
  • Sudden insights, psychic abilities, or a deep sense of interconnectedness
  • Changes in sleep patterns, appetite, and energy levels
  • Extreme sensitivity to light, sound, and other stimuli

Know what I mean? It's a lot. And when you add combat trauma to that mix, it's like throwing gasoline on a fire. The kundalini energy, in its attempt to purify, will inevitably hit all those stored traumas. It'll bring them to the surface, not gently, but with the force of a tidal wave. It's not trying to hurt you; it's trying to heal you, but the healing process can feel like a re-wounding. It's a paradox, honestly. A cruel one, sometimes.

I'll tell you what, there were times I wished it would just stop. I wished I could go back to being numb, to not feeling anything. But once it starts, it's like a train that's left the station. You can't stop it. You can only learn to ride it. And that's where the real work comes in.

Survival Guide for the Spiritual Storm

Okay, so you're in it. What do you do? First, breathe. Seriously. Just breathe. It's the most basic thing, and it's often the hardest when you feel like you're suffocating. Here's what I've learned, what's helped me, and what I've seen help others. This isn't a cure, but it's a way to weather the storm.

Grounding, Grounding, Grounding

When you feel like you're floating away, losing your mind, or vibrating out of your skin, you need to ground yourself. This is your anchor in the storm.

  • Connect with the Earth: Literally. Go outside, take off your shoes, and stand on the grass or dirt. Feel the earth beneath your feet. Visualize roots growing from your feet deep into the ground.
  • Cold Water: A cold shower, splashing cold water on your face, or even holding ice cubes can shock your system back into your body. It's a quick way to interrupt panic and bring you back to the present.
  • Physical Activity: Don't just sit there. Move your body. Go for a walk, do some push-ups, clean your apartment. Physical exertion helps discharge excess energy and brings you back into your physical form.
  • Eat Real Food: Ditch the junk. Eat nourishing, grounding foods - root vegetables, proteins, healthy fats. Your body is working overtime; give it the fuel it needs.
  • Sensory Focus: Pick five things you can see, four things you can feel, three things you can hear, two things you can smell, and one thing you can taste. This pulls you out of your head and into the present moment.

I can't stress this enough. When I was going through it, I'd sometimes just sit on the floor, back against the wall, trying to feel every inch of my body touching the ground. It was the only way to not feel like I was going to explode. It's not a magic bullet, but it helps. It really does.

Professional Support (The Right Kind)

This is tricky, because not every therapist or doctor understands spiritual emergencies. Many will just label you with a mental illness and try to medicate you into oblivion. And sometimes medication is necessary to stabilize you, don't get me wrong. But you need to seek out someone who gets it. Look for therapists who are open to transpersonal psychology, spiritual emergencies, or even The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link). They exist. They're rare, but they exist.

  • Educate Yourself: Read books like When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) or The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link). Understanding the phenomenon can be incredibly validating and reduce the terror.
  • Find a "Spiritual Emergency" Aware Therapist: Ask direct questions. "Do you have experience with spiritual emergencies or kundalini awakenings?" If they look at you blankly, move on. You need someone who won't pathologize your experience but can help you integrate it.
  • Support Groups: Online forums or local groups (if you can find them) of people going through similar experiences can be a lifeline. Just knowing you're not the only one can make a huge difference.

Honestly, I spent a lot of time feeling completely alone. Like I was the only one going through this hell. Don't make that mistake. Reach out. Even if it's just to me, indirectly, through these words. You're not alone in this fight.

The Long Haul: Integration and Healing

This isn't a sprint; it's a marathon. A really, really long, painful marathon. The intense phase of a spiritual emergency can last for months, sometimes years. But it doesn't stay at that peak intensity forever. It waxes and wanes. Your job is to learn to ride the waves, to integrate the experiences, and to build a new foundation for yourself.

This means facing your trauma head-on. The kundalini is bringing it up for a reason - to be seen, felt, and released. It's not easy. It's the hardest thing you'll ever do. But avoiding it just prolongs the suffering. Work with your therapist, practice self-compassion, and allow yourself to feel whatever comes up. You've been strong enough to survive combat; you're strong enough to survive this.

You'll find that as the process unfolds, you'll start to see the world differently. Your priorities will shift. What used to matter might seem trivial. You might develop a deeper sense of purpose, a stronger connection to something larger than yourself. It's not just about surviving; it's about transforming. It's about becoming more whole, more authentic, more truly you than you've ever been. It's a brutal path to liberation, but it is liberation. And you, my friend, deserve that freedom.

It's going to be okay. Not today, maybe not tomorrow, but eventually. Hold on. Keep breathing. Keep fighting. You've got this, even when it feels like you don't. Because you're a veteran. You've been through worse. And this, believe it or not, is leading you home.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
